params.text:
Fire classes and the right extinguisher

Fires are grouped into classes by what is burning, and using the wrong extinguisher can make matters far worse. You are not expected to be an expert, but a basic awareness helps you understand the equipment around you and why it is placed where it is.

  • Class A — solids such as paper, wood and fabric: water or foam.
  • Class B — flammable liquids such as petrol or spirits: foam, carbon dioxide or powder.
  • Class C — flammable gases: the supply should be shut off; powder may be used.
  • Electrical fires: carbon dioxide (CO2) suits live electrical equipment and leaves no residue.

Cooking fires and when not to fight

Two further types matter in care settings. Wet chemical extinguishers are made for Class F fires — cooking oils and deep fat — while dry powder is versatile but creates a choking cloud that is unpleasant and hard to see through indoors. Colour-coded labels help you tell them apart.

Fighting a fire is always a last resort. Only ever tackle a small fire if you have been trained, it is not spreading, you have raised the alarm first, and you have a clear escape route at your back. Never use water on electrical equipment or on a cooking-oil fire. If you are in any doubt at all, get out and leave it to the fire service.

params.question: A small fire has started in live electrical equipment. Which extinguisher is most suitable, and what is the safest overall approach?

answers.text: Carbon dioxide (CO2), but only tackle it if trained, it is not spreading and you have raised the alarm and kept an escape route.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. CO2 suits electrical fires, and fighting any fire is a last resort that depends on training, a contained fire and a clear exit.

answers.text: A water extinguisher, aimed straight at the equipment.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Water conducts electricity and must never be used on live electrical equipment; CO2 is the safer choice.
Incorrect. Wet chemical is designed for cooking-oil (Class F) fires, not electrical equipment.

answers.text: Any extinguisher will do, so grab the nearest one and start spraying.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Using the wrong extinguisher can make a fire worse, and fighting a fire is only ever a last resort when it is safe to do so.
